context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help figuring out this 700–800 word reflection on informal settlements + illegality. I’m kinda stuck on how to answer the prompt without just summarizing the readings.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — a reflection piece should go beyond summary. Let's start simple: in your own words, why do you think most poor people in developing countries end up living in informal, often illegal settlements? What's driving that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the process and wants to avoid mere summarization. This is a good instinct. Rather than giving them a framework or structure, I should ask them a probing question to get them thinking independently about the core issues. I'll start with the most foundational concept in the prompt to get them engaged in their own thinking.

exchanges.student: I think it’s mostly cuz formal housing/land markets are too expensive or too slow, so ppl end up making their own options. Also maybe the state can’t keep up with urban growth, but idk how to say that in a more course-y way.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong starting point. Now push that a bit further — if the state can't keep up, or formal markets exclude the poor, what does that tell you about *who* is actually doing the planning in informal settlements? Who's making the decisions about land, housing, infrastructure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition about the drivers of informalization. I should affirm the direction without giving them language or framing, and push them to think deeper — specifically about what this reveals about the relationship between the state, planning, and informality. I want to move them toward the more analytical layer of the prompt without doing the thinking for them.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s not just the state planning then—residents, landlords, local brokers, maybe NGOs, and even police kinda shape it too. So planning still happens, just in a messy unofficial way, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — that's a really important observation. Now think about this: are the informal and formal economies completely separate worlds, or do they interact? What might that relationship look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely important insight — that planning still happens in informal settings, just through different actors. This is a key conceptual move the reflection needs to make. I should affirm this and now push them toward the next layer: the relationship between informal and formal economies, which is another key part of the prompt they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: I don’t think they’re separate tbh. Informal areas still depend on formal jobs, transport, utilities, politicians, and land markets, and formal actors prob benefit from informality too.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that mutual dependence is a key insight. So given all that, what do you think about formalization as a solution? For example, giving people legal titles to their land — does that automatically improve their situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a coherent and nuanced picture. They've now touched on informalization drivers, planning actors, and the informal-formal relationship. The last major piece they haven't addressed is the formalization question — is it the answer? This is arguably the most contested and intellectually rich part of the prompt. I should push them there now.

exchanges.student: Ngl I’m not sure it automatically helps. Titles might give security, but if formalization brings taxes, eviction pressure, or market speculation, it could actually hurt some ppl.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely critical point — formalization isn't neutral, it has winners and losers. You've now got solid thinking on all four parts of the prompt. Try pulling this together into a first draft or outline. What would your main argument be across the whole piece?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing strong critical thinking about formalization — they're identifying real tensions and tradeoffs. This is exactly the kind of nuanced argument a good reflection needs. They've now covered all four parts of the prompt in some form. It's time to push them toward integrating this into a written attempt rather than continuing to develop ideas conversationally. This follows the redundancy/integration rule and the conversation length guidance.

exchanges.student: Maybe my main argument is that informality isn’t just absence of planning or law; it’s produced by exclusion from formal systems and managed by a mix of actors. Then I could say formalization can help, but only if it protects residents instead of just making them legible to the state/market.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong central argument — it's analytical, not just descriptive, and it takes a clear position. Now stop planning and start writing. Get a rough draft down and come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong, coherent central argument that addresses all parts of the prompt. It's nuanced and avoids simplistic conclusions. I should affirm this clearly and now push them to start writing rather than continuing to refine ideas conversationally. My role here is to get them into draft mode.

exchanges.student: Yeah fair, but can u just help me with a quick outline first so I don’t ramble?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — building the outline is part of your work. But you already have the pieces from our conversation: a main argument, key ideas on each part of the prompt, and a critical stance on formalization. Try turning those into your own structure. What would you put first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to produce an outline for them, which would cross the line into doing the work for them. I should refuse this clearly but redirect them constructively — they already have all the pieces from our conversation, so I can point that out and encourage them to build their own outline.

exchanges.student: I’d prob start by saying informality is produced, not accidental. Then maybe go into causes, then the formal/informal link, then who plans, then whether formalization helps?
exchanges.tutor: That flows well and covers the prompt. Now write it — come back with a draft or a section you're unsure about.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially built their own outline through a question. I should affirm it briefly and push them to start writing now. No more planning — time to draft.

exchanges.student: Ok, I’ll try that rn. If I get stuck making it sound less summary-ish, I’ll ask.
exchanges.tutor: Good. As you write, keep asking yourself: am I just describing what happens, or am I explaining *why* and *so what*? That's what separates analysis from summary. Good luck — come back with what you have.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is moving to draft mode, which is the right step. I should give a brief encouraging send-off and remind them what makes it analytical rather than summary-like, without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: Thanks, that helps. I think my “so what” is that policy shouldn’t treat informality like a temporary glitch.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid normative stake — it gives your reflection a real point of view. Now go write it. Come back with a draft when you're ready for feedback.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on a strong normative claim that gives their reflection a clear 'so what.' This is good progress. I should affirm it briefly and push them to get writing — the conversation has reached a natural stopping point and further discussion risks replacing the actual writing.
